title: Surface Originated Cross-Field Anomalous Transport in Magnetoelectric Multilayers
authors: Jin Cao, Wei Du, Xue-Jin Zhang, Cong Xiao, Qian Niu, Shengyuan A. Yang
abstract: In material systems with slab geometry, the surface contribution to physical responses is commonly expected to diminish rapidly with increasing thickness, giving way to the bulk response. Here, we show that this conventional wisdom is violated in a class of gate-induced responses, including gate-induced orbital and spin magnetization as well as cross-field anomalous thermoelectric transport. We develop a general framework for these effects, which naturally decomposes the total response into surface- and bulk-contributions treated on equal footing. Remarkably, the volume-averaged surface contribution remains finite in the thick-slab limit and exhibits the same thickness scaling as the bulk term. Furthermore, the surface response originates from band geometric quantities distinct from those in the bulk, being constrained solely by surface symmetries. As a result, it can dominate the overall response when the bulk contribution is symmetry-forbidden. Taking MnBi$_2$Te$_4$ multilayers as an example, we predict a strong surface-dominated cross-field anomalous Nernst effect arising from surface Berry curvature, which is readily accessible to experimental detection. These findings reveal a previously overlooked significance of surface response and open a new direction in the study of surface quantum geometry.
summary: This work demonstrates that surface effects in layered quantum materials do not necessarily vanish in the bulk limit, contrary to conventional wisdom. By developing a general framework, the authors show that surface-originated anomalous transport can persist and even dominate the response, particularly when bulk symmetries forbid the effect. This opens a new avenue for designing quantum devices based on surface quantum geometry.
main_problem: The paper challenges the conventional assumption that surface contributions to physical responses in slab geometries diminish rapidly with increasing thickness.
main_result: The surface contribution remains finite and scales with the bulk term even in the thick-slab limit, and it can dominate the overall response when the bulk contribution is symmetry-forbidden.
method: The authors developed a general theoretical framework using linear response theory to decompose the total response into distinct surface- and bulk-contributions.
model_or_system: The study focuses on magnetoelectric multilayers, modeled using a tight-binding Hamiltonian subjected to an external gate electric field ($E_G$).
key_observables: Cross-field anomalous transport coefficients (e.g., anomalous Nernst effect), gate-induced orbital magnetization, and the decomposition of response coefficients ($\chi = \chi_{LP} + \chi_{LH}$).
important_parameters: The thickness of the slab ($L$), the applied gate electric field ($E_G$), and the material system ($	ext{MnBi}_2	ext{Te}_4$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to weak electric fields ($E_G$) and effects linear in $E_G$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic decomposition of responses into surface (LP) and bulk (LH) parts, and show the thickness dependence of the surface contribution, demonstrating saturation to a finite limit.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the problem of surface vs. bulk contributions, develops the general decomposition framework, analyzes the scaling behavior in the thick limit, and finally applies these findings to predict a strong surface-dominated anomalous Nernst effect in $	ext{MnBi}_2	ext{Te}_4$.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter transport, the rigorous mathematical decomposition of total response into distinct, non-trivial surface and bulk components provides a powerful, generalizable theoretical tool for analyzing boundary effects in quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A multilayer system under gate field EG. The layer here may correspond to either real atomic sites or a lattice chosen for discretizing the low-energy model of the material system.

FIG. 2. Schematics of (a) gate-field-induced orbital magneti- zation ˜ M and (b) cross-field anomalous thermoelectric trans- port. The responses consist of surface (LP) and bulk (LH) contributions. The symbols in the square brackets highlight their dependence on distinct band geometric quantities.

FIG. 4. (a) Calculated cross-field anomalous Hall coefficient αLP 0,3D for MnBi2Te4 multilayers as a function of L (even- number of layers), at µ = 0.1 eV. The inset shows the layer- resolved contribution to αLP 0,3D for the case with L = 20. The star indicates the value for L →∞limit. (b) Same as (a), but for the Nernst response αLP 1,3D.

FIG. 3. (a) Band structure of MnBi2Te4 multilayer model with a thickness of 20 septuple layers. The red color highlights the surface bands. (b) Calculated χLP 3D ≡χLP/L (at µ = 0) as a function of the slab thickness L (taking even-number of layers). The star indicates the value in the L →∞limit. The solid curve serves as a guide to the eyes.
